text:
University College Hospital 

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery 

Clinic Date: 28 December 2023 

Dr Susan  
University College Hospital  
National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery, London WC1N 3BG  

Dear Dr Susan,  

William Harris, DOB: 15-02-1965, Hospital No: H563829 NHS No. 9273645810  
Temporary Accommodation, Flat 3, 22 Brookside Lane, London, EC1V 9BT 

Summary/ Diagnoses: Generalised epilepsy/awaiting relocation from temporary accommodation with crowded living conditions/low mood symptoms under primary care review/vitamin D insufficiency/ongoing smoking. 

Medication: Sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily.  
Clobazam 10 mg at night (intermittent use for clusters).  
Cholecalciferol 1000 IU daily.  
Omeprazole 20 mg daily. 

I reviewed William Harris in clinic today. He attended alone and reported ongoing stress related to housing. He is currently in temporary accommodation and awaiting relocation; he describes crowded living conditions with limited privacy and irregular sleep due to noise from other residents. He feels this has affected his general wellbeing and concentration. He remains independent in activities of daily living, walks without aids, and has had no recent injuries. Appetite is fair; weight stable by report. He smokes approximately 5–7 cigarettes per day and drinks alcohol rarely. No recreational drug use reported. 

Seizures: He has a known diagnosis of generalised epilepsy. He keeps a phone diary and reports that his last seizure on 26/Sep occurred in the early morning, described as a generalised tonic–clonic seizure with tongue bite and post-ictal confusion for around 45 minutes. He did not require hospital attendance. He attributes a probable trigger to sleep deprivation the night prior due to noise in the accommodation. Prior to this, he had been seizure-free for several months. No absence episodes or myoclonus reported recently. There is no current evidence of focal features. 

Examination: Alert, oriented, speech fluent. Cranial nerves normal. Motor examination normal tone and power throughout. Coordination and gait normal. No focal neurological deficits. 

Investigations: Last routine bloods (Oct 2023) showed normal FBC and U&Es; LFTs within reference range; vitamin D borderline low at 23 nmol/L (on supplementation). No recent antiseizure medication levels requested. 

Plan:  
- Continue sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily.  
- Clobazam 10 mg nocte to be used only for short-term cluster prevention as previously agreed.  
- Reinforce seizure self-management: importance of regular sleep and adherence to medication.  
- Safety advice reiterated including bathing supervision where possible, cooking precautions, and avoidance of heights.  
- Provide letter supporting expedited relocation given the impact of crowded living conditions on sleep and seizure control.  
- Check vitamin D level at next blood draw (request sent to practice).  
- Smoking cessation support signposted via local services.  
- Follow-up in neurology clinic in 6 months, or sooner if further generalised seizures occur. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: He keeps a phone diary and reports that his last seizure on 26/Sep occurred in the early morning... Prior to this, he had been seizure-free for several months.

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter states the patient had a last seizure on 26 September, described as a generalized tonic–clonic seizure, and prior to this he had been seizure-free for several months. Clinic date is 28 December 2023, which is about 3 months after the last seizure. The 6‑month seizure‑free criterion is not met, so he cannot be categorized as seizure free. There is no explicit ongoing frequency (e.g., per week/month), only that there was a single seizure after several months seizure freedom. Without a clear rate over a defined period, a numeric frequency cannot be determined. Therefore, per instruction, return "unknown."
seizure_frequency_number: He keeps a phone diary and reports that his last seizure on 26/Sep occurred in the early morning... Prior to this, he had been seizure-free for several months.
